evidence.scientific_clarity: Stacked bars clearly show 4-class proportions across 5 conditions; y-axis uses percent labels 0–100%; brief's claim is fully conveyed.
evidence.typography: pdf.fonttype defaults to 3 (Type 3 bitmap), not 42; pdffonts confirms 'BMQQDV+DejaVuSans Type 3' — text is not editable/selectable; DejaVu Sans default font used.
evidence.palette: Seaborn-derived blue/green/red/orange palette; red (#C44E52) and green (#55A868) are adjacent in stacked bars, problematic for deuteranopia/protanopia; not fully colorblind-safe.
evidence.layout_density: Legend placed outside plot area (bbox_to_anchor=(1.01,1)) which wastes right-side space; plot area itself is well-used with no overlapping labels; y-range 0–1 is correct.
evidence.reviewer_readiness: Caption matches figure content and mentions specific conditions (Cond2–Cond3, Cond5, Cond4); legend is outside the axes which is acceptable but slightly awkward; no gridlines may hinder reading stacked values.
evidence.vector_fidelity: PDF is 12KB and uses Type 3 fonts (bitmap glyphs), not Type 1/TrueType with fonttype=42; text is not selectable in the PDF; script does not set pdf.fonttype=42.
evidence.file_format: figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg, caption.tex, and gen_figure.py all exist and are non-trivial (PDF 12KB, PNG 23KB, SVG 30KB).
evidence.caption_quality: Caption names all four classes with colors, states each bar sums to 100%, identifies specific conditions where Class C dominates and where distribution is most even, and cites data source.

overall_strengths: All required output files are present and non-trivial; figure.svg also included as a bonus.
Caption is specific and informative, naming dominant classes per condition and citing the data source.
overall_weaknesses: pdf.fonttype is not set to 42, resulting in Type 3 bitmap fonts in the PDF — text is not selectable or editable by journal production.
Red/green color pair (#C44E52 and #55A868) in adjacent stacked segments is not safe for red-green colorblind readers; a colorblind-safe palette (e.g., Wong or Okabe-Ito) should be used.
